audit_status.source_base: Greek Acts text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Corinth, Priscilla/Aquila, tentmaking, synagogue opposition, Crispus, vision, Gallio, Cenchreae, Ephesus, Apollos instructed more accurately.

verses.restored: After these things, departing from Athens, he came to Corinth.
He found a certain Judean named Aquila, Pontian by birth, recently come from Italy with Priscilla his wife, because Claudius had ordered all the Judeans to depart from Rome; he came to them.
Because he was of the same trade, he remained with them and worked; for by trade they were tentmakers.
He was reasoning in the synagogue every Sabbath and persuading Judeans and Greeks.
When Silas and Timothy came down from Macedonia, Paul was pressed by the word, testifying to the Judeans that Jesus is the Messiah.
When they opposed and blasphemed, he shook out his garments and said to them, Your blood be upon your head; I am clean. From now I will go to the nations.
Departing from there, he entered the house of a certain Titius Justus, a reverer of the Divine, whose house was adjoining the synagogue.
Crispus, the synagogue ruler, trusted the Lord with his whole house, and many of the Corinthians hearing were trusting and being baptized.
The Lord said to Paul through a vision in the night, Do not fear, but speak and do not be silent,
because I am with you, and no one will lay a hand on you to harm you, because there is much people for me in this city.
He sat there a year and six months, teaching the word of the Divine among them.
But when Gallio was proconsul of Achaia, the Judeans rose with one accord against Paul and brought him to the judgment seat,
saying, This one persuades humans to revere the Divine contrary to the Law.
As Paul was about to open his mouth, Gallio said to the Judeans, If indeed it were some wrongdoing or wicked crime, O Judeans, according to reason I would put up with you.
But if it is questions about a word and names and your own Law, see to it yourselves; I am not willing to be judge of these things.
He drove them from the judgment seat.
They all seized Sosthenes, the synagogue ruler, and beat him before the judgment seat, and none of these things mattered to Gallio.
Paul, remaining many days longer, said farewell to the siblings and sailed away to Syria, and with him Priscilla and Aquila. In Cenchreae he cut his hair, for he had a vow.
They came to Ephesus, and he left them there. He himself entered the synagogue and reasoned with the Judeans.
When they asked him to remain for a longer time, he did not consent,
but saying farewell and saying, I will return to you again, the Divine willing, he set sail from Ephesus.
Coming down to Caesarea, going up and greeting the assembly, he went down to Antioch.
After spending some time, he departed, passing through the Galatian region and Phrygia in order, strengthening all the disciples.
A certain Judean named Apollos, Alexandrian by birth, an eloquent man, powerful in the Scriptures, came to Ephesus.
This one had been instructed in the Way of the Lord, and being fervent in spirit, he was speaking and teaching accurately the things concerning Jesus, knowing only the baptism of John.
He began to speak boldly in the synagogue. Hearing him, Priscilla and Aquila took him aside and explained to him the Way of the Divine more accurately.
When he wished to pass through to Achaia, the siblings encouraged him and wrote to the disciples to welcome him. Arriving, he greatly helped those who through grace had trusted,
for he was powerfully refuting the Judeans publicly, showing through the Scriptures that Jesus is the Messiah.
